query: mast cells in normal and inflamed dental pulp

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Structure and Function -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The pulp mass is a highly vascularized and innervated mass of connective tissue that resides within a spaced called the pulp chamber. Various cell types characterize this tissue, including fibroblasts, odontoblasts, histiocytes, macrophages, mast cells, and plasma cells. It also contains an extracellular matrix composed of collagenous fibers and ground substance. Given that the pulp forms the layer of dentin evenly in every direction, it effectively shapes itself into miniaturization of the tooth and reflects the external form of the enamel. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Polymorphonuclear leukocytes play a significant role in the host response, as they secrete cytoplastic granules with enzymatic properties. Although the primary responsibility of PMNs is to protect the host, they can cause structural damage to tissue cells and extracellular matrices. [11] Thus, we can see how the accumulation of PMNs plays a significant role in the destruction of periapical tissues during the acute phase of AP. [7] [11] Other host cells that play crucial roles are lymphocytes, macrophages, and osteoclasts.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: The physiology of the pulp can change over time due to the aging process, resulting in distinct phenotypic differences. Compromised circulation and innervation occurs due to increased deposition of secondary dentin at the apical root, constricting the apical foramen. Blood vessels, lymphatic vessels, and nerves entering aged pulp demonstrate arteriosclerotic changes, degenerative changes, and progressive mineralization of the nerve sheath, respectively. The nerve death that occurs in aged pulp explains why aged teeth are frequently painless. Remaining odontoblasts become smaller and have a flattened morphology. Despite there being a profound reduction in cell density (about half from 20 to 70 years old), another characteristic of aged pulp is an increase in fibrosis and collagenous fibers. Due to the reduction in fibroblasts, this apparent increase is not due to increased production of collagenous fibers, but rather reflects the sheaths of connective tissue that remain after the death of nerves and blood vessels to become part of the fibrous matrix. [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Many methods can help to discriminate between reversible and irreversible pulpitis. Sensibility tests leverage the innervation of the pulp to assess pulpal health by examining the sensory response of a tooth to an outside stimulus. One common example is a cold test, in which a cold stimulus, such as a cotton pellet cooled with dry ice, is applied to the tooth in question. In a healthy tooth, a mild response should occur that persists for no longer than 1 to 2 seconds after removal of the stimulus. [14] The same stimulus applied to a severely and irreversibly inflamed tooth results in pain that are significantly sharper, lingering for more than 30 seconds after removal of the stimulus. Other sensibility tests include mechanical ones, such as tapping the crown of the tooth or asking the patient to bite on something hard, and electric pulp testing, which measures the ability of neurons innervating the pulp to generate an action potential. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Severe inflammatory reactions can lead to pulpal cell injury, potentially compromising pulpal vitality and survivability. Although the infection activates an immune response, immune cells and molecules are unable to effectively penetrate dentin to remove pathogens due to the narrow apical foramen of the tooth and the hard enamel surrounding the internal structures. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: As previously stated, the host response includes various cells, mediators, effector molecules, and antibodies. The cellular elements include polymorphonuclear leukocytes (PMN), lymphocytes, monocytes/macrophages, and plasma cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Bone Marrow -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Granulomas
retrieved_docs.content: Macrophage infiltration and granulomatous inflammation often pose diagnostic challenges. Exclusion of CD68-expressing mast cells mimicking granulomas is essential. Granulomas may be associated with diseases like HL but do not necessarily indicate direct lymphomatous bone marrow infiltration. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Structure -- Lamina Propria
retrieved_docs.content: The principal cell found in the lamina propria is the fibroblast, which undertakes essential functions. It participates in the synthesis and replenishment of the connective fibers and the amorphous substance and takes part in wound healing, where the number of fibroblasts increases. In some conditions, such as drug-induced gingival overgrowth, the inducing drugs trigger the activation and proliferation of the gingival tissues' fibroblasts, resulting in an increased secretion of the glycosaminoglycan of the amorphous substance [23] . Macrophages mainly participate in phagocytic activities and also stimulate fibroblast proliferation during wound healing. Finally, mast cells are also found in the connective tissue of the lamina propria. Their distinctive feature is cytoplasmatic granules containing heparin and histamine, the latter being known for initiating vascular changes in the inflammatory process. The two main fibers found in the connective tissue of the lamina propria are collagen and elastin, where collagen fibers type I and III are the principal ones.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Facial Infection of Dental Origin: A Guide for the Medical Practitioner -- Etiology -- Pulp Involvement
retrieved_docs.content: The dental pulp can be irreversibly damaged due to persistent inflammation, leading to constant, spontaneous, and acute dental pain that is poorly localized. Irreversible pulpitis is a primary reason patients present to the emergency department. [7] Irreversible pulpitis must be referred to a dentist, as the standard of care is partial or complete pulp removal. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Oral Gingiva -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The gingival connective tissue (also known as the lamina propria) is composed of collagen fibers, cells, and ground substance. Cells make up 5% of the gingival connective tissue, while collagen fibers and ground substance make up 60% and 35%, respectively. The different cell types present in gingival connective tissue include fibroblasts, mast cells, macrophages, and inflammatory cells. Fibroblasts are the predominant cell type and are responsible for the formation of the collagen fibers and ground substance found in connective tissue. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Mast Cells -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Mast cells are found in loose (areolar) connective tissue throughout the body, in virtually every organ. They play an important role in inducing the inflammatory cascade. Innate or adaptive immune mechanisms can induce the mast cell to degranulate, releasing inflammatory mediators into the extracellular space. Mast cells are associated with many pathologies, including type I hypersensitivity reactions, mastocytosis, mast cell activation syndrome, and urticaria. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Carotidynia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The vessel wall may show low-grade chronic active inflammation and mild fibrosis in patients with carotidynia on histological examination. [18] The proliferation of small blood vessels and prominent endothelial cells embedded in a fibromyxoid stroma are noted. [18] Other cell types include predominant lymphocytes, fibroblasts, mast cells, and a few polymorphonuclear leukocytes. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Pathophysiology -- Pulpitis and Pulp Necrosis
retrieved_docs.content: The pulp is the center part of a tooth directly beneath the dentin layer, which contains nerves and blood vessels. Bacterial decay can erode through the enamel and dentin layers and enter the pulp; this causes an infection of this tissue, resulting in pulpitis. The microbes causing pulpitis continue to be an area of study, but it is predominantly thought that anaerobic bacteria are the leading cause of pulp infections. [35] Pulp necrosis results when the pulpitis infection progresses and causes irreversible damage and cell death.

This analysis can assist in determining the time of injury, providing insights to law enforcement. Histopathology of an injury can provide vital information for any potential investigation. [15] [16] [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is identified as a pathological finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating the presence of infiltrating malignant cells within the affected bone tissue. Likewise, Tumor cells, malignant are recognized as the abnormal cellular component that characterizes سرطانة العظم, reflecting the disease’s neoplastic nature. Together, these findings underscore that both the infiltrative pattern of malignant cells and the presence of malignant tumor cells are key diagnostic features of سرطانة العظم, aiding clinicians in confirming the diagnosis and guiding appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2894
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding that indicates the presence of سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). The detection of malignant cellular infiltrate in tissue samples signals that cancerous cells have invaded the bone, serving as an important diagnostic marker for confirming bone malignancy and guiding subsequent therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2407
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a diagnostic finding associated with both السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer) and سرطانة العظم (bone cancer), indicating the presence of malignant disease in these organs. In contrast, Tumor cells, benign are explicitly noted as not being findings of either السرطان البنكرياسي or سرطانة العظم, reflecting that benign tumor cells do not signify these malignant conditions. Conversely, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as abnormal cells of disease for both السرطان البنكرياسي and سرطانة العظم, underscoring their role as the pathological cellular component characteristic of these cancers. This distinction between malignant infiltrates, benign tumor cells, and malignant tumor cells is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate patient management in cases of pancreatic and bone malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 204

Metastatic carcinoma to a lymph node of the head and neck and metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of the head and neck are both characterized as cancerous tumors; the latter is an inverse ISA of squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck (سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق) and carries the specific morphology of metastatic squamous cell carcinoma (سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية). The non‑hematologic malignant diseases group (الأمراض غير الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)) functions as a container for secondary malignant tumors in other specified sites, malignant tumors of the upper jaw (ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي), and malignant tumor of the sublingual gland, and it is itself a member of the broader metastatic cancer category (السرطان النقيلي). This SMQ group is also listed as a member of both pancreatic cancer and melanomas of the skin, indicating that these disease families belong to the non‑hematologic malignant disease spectrum. Bone cancer (سرطان العظم)

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, are identified as abnormal cells of disease and are classified under the category “cancer; other and unspecified primary,” indicating that these cells represent a form of cancer for which the primary site cannot be determined. This classification underscores the diagnostic uncertainty surrounding such tumor cells and highlights the need for further pathological evaluation to clarify their nature and guide appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1989
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids for local oral treatment and other agents for local oral treatment in ATC are both classified as stomatological preparations, indicating that each is a type of medication formulated for use within the oral cavity to manage dental or mucosal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2276
retrieved_docs.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية) encompasses several distinct conditions, including التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). Each of these diseases is characterized by an inappropriate immune response that targets the body’s own tissues, leading to joint inflammation, destruction of red blood cells, or inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities share a common autoimmune basis aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and applying immunomodulatory therapies across this spectrum of immune‑mediated illnesses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is a finding of disease and is categorized as cancer; other and unspecified primary, indicating that the presence of malignant cellular infiltrate reflects an underlying malignant neoplasm of unknown primary origin. Recognizing this finding is essential for guiding further diagnostic work‑up and management of patients with cancers where the primary site cannot be identified.
